title: How somatic experiencing releases what words cannot reach
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
You can't think your way out of a trauma response 

 I spent years trying to talk my way to healing. I sat in therapists' offices, told my story over and...
htmlContent:
You can't think your way out of a trauma response

I spent years trying to talk my way to healing. I sat in therapists' offices, told my story over and over, analyzed my childhood, reframed my thoughts, journaled until my hand cramped. And you know what? I got smarter about my trauma. I could name every defense mechanism, every attachment style, every cognitive distortion. But my body still held the same tension. I still woke up with my jaw clenched. I still froze when someone raised their voice. I still felt like a frightened animal trapped inside a rational adult's life.

Here's the thing. The parts of us that store trauma don't speak in words. They speak in sensation. In temperature changes. In muscle tension. In that hollow feeling in your chest when something reminds you of a danger that's long gone. Bessel van der Kolk, the psychiatrist who wrote "The Body Keeps the Score," spent decades showing us that trauma isn't just a story in your head. It's a physiological event that gets lodged in your nervous system, in your fascia, in the way your breath shortens when you're triggered. You can't reason your way out of a body that's learned to be afraid.

That's where somatic experiencing comes in. It's a method developed by Peter Levine, a psychologist who noticed something crucial while observing animals in the wild. Prey animals get chased, nearly killed, and then minutes later they're shaking it off and grazing peacefully. They don't develop PTSD. Why? Because they complete the stress cycle physically. They tremble, they run, they shake, they discharge the energy that got mobilized for survival. We humans, we override those impulses. We freeze. We brace. We tell ourselves to calm down. We intellectualize. And the energy stays locked in our tissues, waiting for a chance to finish what it started.

I'm not saying talk therapy is useless. I've benefited from it immensely. But there's a limit to what words can reach. Your verbal brain, your neocortex, that's maybe 20% of your nervous system. The other 80% is running beneath language, beneath conscious thought, beneath the stories you tell yourself about who you are. That's where trauma lives. And that's where somatic experiencing goes to work.

The language your body actually speaks

Somatic experiencing doesn't ask you to relive your trauma. Thank God for that. I've done exposure therapy. I've been asked to recount the worst moments of my life in detail, over and over, until they supposedly lost their power. It didn't work for me. It just retraumatized me. Levine's approach is different. It's gentler. It's slower. It asks a deceptively simple question: What are you noticing in your body right now?

Not what you think about what happened. Not the story. Not the meaning. Just the raw sensation. The heat in your face. The tightness in your throat. The way your stomach drops when you remember something you'd rather forget. You track these sensations without trying to change them. Without judging them. Without analyzing them. You just stay with them, like a curious observer, and you let your nervous system do what it's been trying to do since the trauma happened.

Stay with me here. This is where it gets weird and beautiful. When you stay with a sensation long enough, something shifts. The tightness might start to vibrate. The heat might spread or cool. You might feel an impulse to stretch, to shake, to cry, to yawn. These are not random. These are your body's attempts to complete the incomplete stress response. That shaking? That's the same thing you see in a deer after it escapes a predator. That yawning? That's a parasympathetic reset. That urge to push your hands against the wall? That's your body finishing a fight response that never got to happen.

I remember the first time I did this in a session. I was lying on a mat, and my therapist asked me to notice a place of tension in my body. I felt it in my chest, this cold, hard knot. She asked me to describe it without words. Just point to where it was. Just let my hand hover over it. Just notice if it had a shape, a color, a temperature. I started crying before I could say anything. Not sad crying. Not memory crying. Just my body saying, finally, someone is paying attention to me. Someone is listening to the language I've been speaking for thirty years.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link). Levine wrote it for people who want to understand the science behind this work. It's not a replacement for a trained practitioner, but it's a good map of the territory.

What happens when you stop trying to fix yourself

This is the part that trips people up. Including me, honestly. We're so conditioned to believe that healing means effort, struggle, pushing through. We think if we're not crying on a yoga mat or screaming into a pillow or doing some dramatic release, we're not doing it right. But somatic experiencing asks you to do something counterintuitive. It asks you to slow down. To pendulate between discomfort and safety. To touch the edge of your trauma without diving in.

Think about it. Your nervous system has been in survival mode for years, maybe decades. It's hypervigilant. It's braced for impact. It's convinced that if it relaxes for one second, something terrible will happen. And it's not wrong, in a way. That hypervigilance kept you alive. It helped you detect danger before danger detected you. But now it's keeping you trapped. You can't tell your nervous system to calm down with words. It doesn't speak English. It speaks sensation, rhythm, temperature, pressure, movement.

So you start small. You notice the feeling of your feet on the floor. You notice the weight of your body against the chair. You notice the air moving in and out of your nostrils. You do this for thirty seconds. Then a minute. Then you notice that your shoulders dropped a millimeter. That your breath got a little deeper. That the knot in your stomach loosened just slightly. That's not nothing. That's your nervous system learning, in real time, that it's safe enough to soften.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). The deep pressure stimulation mimics the feeling of being held, which can help your parasympathetic nervous system engage. It's not a cure. But it's a tool. And sometimes a tool is what you need to start feeling safe enough to feel what you've been avoiding.

I've had sessions where the only thing that happened was I noticed my breathing was shallow and then I took one deeper breath. That was it. That was the work. And my therapist celebrated that like I'd climbed a mountain. Because in a way, I had. I'd done the hardest thing a traumatized nervous system can do. I'd let myself experience a moment of safety without bracing for the other shoe to drop.

Wild, right? That something so small can be so . But that's the thing about trauma. It's not just the big events. It's the way your body learned to hold itself in a state of constant preparation. It's the way you stopped feeling your own skin. It's the way you forgot what it felt like to be truly at rest. Healing isn't about getting rid of the trauma. It's about giving your body enough safety to come out of hiding.

The limits of words and the wisdom of sensation

I want to be honest with you about something. Somatic experiencing isn't magic. It's not a quick fix. It's not going to make your trauma disappear. Trauma doesn't disappear. It integrates. It transforms. It becomes something you can carry without it destroying you. But that takes time. That takes patience. That takes a willingness to feel things you've spent your whole life trying not to feel.

And here's the uncomfortable truth. The wellness industry has co-opted a lot of this language. They've turned "somatic" into a buzzword. They've sold you the idea that if you just buy the right crystal, do the right breathwork, attend the right retreat, you'll be healed. That's not how it works. Healing is not a product. It's not something you can purchase. It's something you have to live through, day by day, sensation by sensation, moment by moment.

Gabor Mate, the physician who wrote about the connection between stress and illness, said something that stuck with me. He said that trauma isn't what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happens to you. It's the wound that forms in the absence of what you needed. And that wound doesn't heal by being ignored or bypassed. It heals by being witnessed. By being felt. By being allowed to exist without being fixed.

A book that connects the dots between stress and illness is When the Body Says No (paid link). It's not a somatic experiencing manual, but it's a powerful look at how our bodies hold what we can't express. It might help you understand why your body has been saying no for so long, even when your mouth kept saying yes.

I'll leave you with this. The next time you feel triggered, the next time you feel that familiar tightness or numbness or panic rising, try something different. Instead of reaching for a thought or an explanation or a story, just notice. Notice what's happening in your body. Notice without trying to change it. Notice without judging it. Notice without telling yourself you should be over this by now. Just notice. And see what happens.

Your body has been waiting for you to listen. It's been speaking this whole time. You just haven't known the language. But you're learning now. And that's enough. That's more than enough.
